Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd just... not. Most of my 26 hours of studying happened in bursts when I felt the pressure building up. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to watch videos and look at diagrams, but organizing when to do that was never my strong suit. The anxiety made it hard to even start sometimes.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly realize something was due and panic-study for hours. Regular schedules felt impossible to maintain with everything else going on in my head. When I'm scared about failing, paradoxically I sometimes just freeze instead of working consistently.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd log in when I remembered an assignment was coming up or when I got that sinking feeling that I'd missed something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements that might affect my grade. It wasn't really a habit, more like damage control.
Q4: Not really, no. I logged in maybe 30 times over the course, which isn't much. There was never a point where it felt natural or automatic. I'd have to force myself to open the LMS, and even then I'd often just look around briefly and leave without doing much. The routine never stuck.
Q5: The videos were the most useful for me since I learn visually. I watched quite a bit of video content, over 200 minutes total. Seeing concepts explained visually helped them stick better than reading walls of text. When a video was well-made, I could actually understand things that seemed impossible from the textbook alone.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums completely. I only posted twice the whole course and never really interacted with other students there. The idea of putting my thoughts out there for others to judge made my anxiety spike. I also delayed a lot of readings, especially the dense theoretical ones that didn't have visual components.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to passing the course. If it directly related to an assignment or exam, I'd engage with it. Anything that felt abstract or disconnected from my grade just didn't register as important enough to overcome the mental barrier of starting.
Q8: Optional activities might as well have been invisible to me. When something was required, I had external pressure forcing me to do it, even if I left it until the last minute. Optional stuff? My brain just categorized it as 'not necessary for survival' and I never got around to it. The fear of failing required tasks was the only thing that motivated me.
Q9: Yeah, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up online. I'd screenshot diagrams and study them on my phone later. I'd download PDFs and read them without being connected. Sometimes I'd just stare at my handwritten notes from the few lectures I attended. A lot of my visual learning happened with physical materials spread out on my desk.
Q10: There were definitely times I was studying but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd watch a video once, then replay concepts in my head for days. I'd study from downloaded materials on the bus or in bed. Sometimes I'd work through problems on paper using concepts from the course without logging in at all. My exam score came partly from that invisible work.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd access something once, download it or screenshot it, and then work with it offline for a while. Especially the visual materials like diagrams and charts. It was easier to study from my phone's gallery than to keep logging into the LMS every time. One access could mean hours of actual study time.
Q12: There were several points where engaging became really hard. When my anxiety peaked, I'd avoid the LMS entirely because even looking at it reminded me of everything I was behind on. Mid-semester was particularly rough when assignments piled up. The fear of seeing bad feedback or missed deadlines made me avoid logging in altogether.
Q13: All of those things hit me at various points. The workload felt overwhelming sometimes, and when I was confused about something, I'd just shut down instead of seeking help. Fatigue from anxiety-related sleep issues made it hard to focus. I don't have a job or many responsibilities outside school, but my mental state created its own competing demands.
Q14: Plenty of times. I'd open a reading and my eyes would move across the words but nothing would register. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be racing about other worries. Sometimes I'd spend an hour 'studying' and realize I couldn't remember a single thing. Being logged in didn't mean I was actually present.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially assignments. My assignment scores were pretty mediocre because of this. I knew they were important, but the anxiety about doing them poorly made me avoid starting. It's like my brain preferred the vague fear of 'I should do this' over the concrete fear of 'what if I do this and it's terrible.'
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd ignore something for weeks, then scramble in the final hours. My LMS usage probably spiked dramatically right before due dates. Without deadlines, I don't think I would have completed much at all. They were stressful but necessary for someone like me.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. When it was positive, it helped a little. But negative feedback or even constructive criticism would send my anxiety through the roof and make me avoid that type of work even more. After the CBT session, I tried to process feedback differently, but it was still hard not to take it personally.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty standard, I guess. Having clear deadlines helped because otherwise I'd never start anything. But the structure didn't really account for someone who struggles with consistent engagement. I needed more scaffolding or check-ins, but I also would have dreaded those, so I don't know what would have helped.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier for me as a visual learner. But the discussion requirements made it harder because I had zero motivation to interact with peers online. The lack of structured weekly milestones meant I could drift for too long without consequences, which wasn't good for someone like me who needs external pressure.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways - logging in frequently out of anxiety, checking things repeatedly, watching videos without absorbing them. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just spinning your wheels, going through motions without actually producing work. Fear can make you very active but not very effective.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My LMS engagement was low, but I passed with a decent exam score. I did a lot of studying offline with downloaded materials. Some people just don't need to be constantly logged in to learn. The system can't see what happens with printed notes or materials saved to your phone.
Q22: Course logs can show when and how often you access things, which videos you watch, what you submit. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through motions. They definitely can't capture offline study, mental state, or whether the content is actually sinking in.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. For me, true engagement is rare. Most of the time I'm just trying to survive and pass. Real engagement would mean I'm curious and motivated, not just scared of failing. I don't think I was truly engaged much this course.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much fear and anxiety drove my behavior. It can't show the hours I spent worrying instead of working, or the offline studying I did. It can't capture how the CBT session affected my approach, or how my high resilience helped me scrape through despite everything. The emotional reality behind the data is completely invisible.
